Practical deployment solutions to mitigate harm for subgroups from models with differential performance have yet to be established. This paper will build on existing work that considers a selective deployment approach where poorly performing subgroups are excluded from deployments. Alternatively, the proposed \u2018mitigated deployment\u2019 strategy requires safety nets to be built into clinical workflows to safeguard under-represented groups in a universal deployment. This approach relies on human\u2013artificial intelligence collaboration and postmarket evaluation to continually improve model performance across subgroups with real-world data. Using a real-world case study, the benefits and limitations of mitigated deployment are explored. This will add to the tools available to healthcare organisations when considering how to safely deploy models with differential performance across subgroups.<\/jats:p>","DOI":"10.1136\/bmjhci-2024-101363","type":"journal-article","created":{"date-parts":[[2025,7,14]],"date-time":"2025-07-14T03:40:24Z","timestamp":1752464424000},"page":"e101363","update-policy":"https:\/\/doi.org\/10.1136\/crossmarkpolicy","source":"Crossref","is-referenced-by-count":1,"title":["Mitigated
